cases.message: When is my credit card due?
cases.norm: when is my credit card due

reply.text: Soonest: BPI Card, due Mon, Jul 27 (moved to the next banking day), balance ₱12,000. Pay in full to stay interest free, or at least the ₱600 minimum. Paying only the minimum adds about ₱360 interest next month.

Soonest: BPI Card, due Mon, Jul 27 (moved to the next banking day), balance ₱12,000. Pay in full to stay interest free, or at least the ₱600 minimum. Paying only the minimum adds about ₱360 interest next month.
No debts to pay, nice. Debt free is a strong place to be.

cases.norm: when will i be debt free

reply.text: Paying current minimums, you are debt free around Fri, Sep 1 with about ₱14,195 total interest. Try asking "if I add 1000 a month" to see how much sooner you finish.

Paying current minimums, you are debt free around Fri, Sep 1 with about ₱14,195 total interest. Adding ₱1,000 a month moves that to Wed, Dec 1 and cuts interest to about ₱8,153. Small extra, big difference.

cases.message: when will i be debt free
cases.norm: when will i be debt free

reply.text: At the current minimums, interest is outpacing your payments, so the balance is not going down. Paying more than the minimum, even a little, is what turns it around. Try "if I add 1000 a month" to see the difference.

At the current minimums, interest is outpacing your payments, so the balance is not going down. Paying more than the minimum, even a little, is what turns it around. Try "if I add 1000 a month" to see the difference.

At the current minimums, interest is outpacing your payments, so the balance is not going down. But adding ₱6,000 a month gets you to debt free around Sat, Jan 1. Paying more than the minimum is the way out.

cases.norm: what is my take home pay

reply.text: To turn a gross salary into net, the Take-home pay calculator in Tools does it properly, with SSS, PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG, tax, and allowances, and can show it per cutoff, monthly, or yearly.
For 13th month pay, the calculator in Tools figures what you should get, prorated for months worked, and shows the tax-free part up to the 90,000 ceiling.
For your monthly SSS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG, the Contribution checker in Tools shows what comes out of your pay, what your employer adds, and the total, for any salary.
To see the real cost of a loan, the Loan calculator in Tools shows the monthly payment, total interest, and the true effective rate, so an add-on quote cannot hide how much it really costs.

guardrail.reply: I do not give investment advice, that needs a licensed professional. What I can do is show you exactly how much you can set aside, so you decide with clear numbers. Want your safe to spend, or how a goal is pacing?
